# Transcrição: 02-Why You Can't Be Steve Jobs

- **Fonte:** 02-Why You Can't Be Steve Jobs
- **Duração:** 04:25
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:01]

One of the most common refrains I hear from people in the corporate world is simply, why do I have to put myself into my presentation? Why can't my content stand alone? The obvious answer is that if you don't put yourself, if you don't put your personality, if you don't emotionally connect with your audience in some way, your content is most certainly going to be round, white, and flavorless. It's going to be the typical corporate communication that I see every single day. It's going to be the same thing that everyone else does. That's the easy answer. But what I'm often asked is, how come Steve Jobs was able to stand on a stage and talk about an iPhone without talking about himself and everyone thinks he was brilliant? And I always laugh when I hear that. The problem is that you're not Steve Jobs. The assumption that these people make is that Steve Jobs on the stage was a blank slate presenting a product when nothing could be further from the case. Steve Jobs was a human being whose story we all knew intimately. We understand that this was a man who founded Apple with partners and that eventually Apple ejected him from the company. He went on to start a new company and then eventually with the use of that new company, he came back and he saved Apple. Most of us knew that Steve Jobs was the kind of guy who was very in touch with himself, that he went to India to find himself, that he believed in spiritualism, that he wore the same clothes every single day. We knew Steve Jobs when he was presenting the iPhone. He didn't have to talk about himself because we already knew who he was. If you are lucky enough to be a known quantity like Steve Jobs, if you are presenting a marketing pitch and everyone in the room knows who you are, understands your beliefs, understands your story, knows where you came from and where you're going, by all means, don't worry about talking about yourself. Don't worry about your own story. But how many of you are Steve Jobs? How many of you are a known quantity? How many of you can walk into a room and people look at you and say, oh, I know your story. I know your belief system. I know how you live. I know who you are. Very few of us operate in that world. Most all of us are unknown quantities to our audiences. The things that our audiences know about us is limited or nonexistent. That is why when you are going to your customers, your investors, your clients, your team, you

## [03:01 - 04:25]

must include yourself in everything that you do. Your goal is not to present content. If you think your goal is to present content, you are seeking to be round, white, and flavorless. You're looking to be forgettable and boring. People are not inspired by content. They're inspired by people. They're inspired by the person standing before us. We're inspired by Steve Jobs because we know his story. We understand his affinity for design. We understand the struggle that he underwent to return to the company he founded. You are a mystery. You are an enigma. You must make yourself known. You must emotionally connect with your audience so that they believe in you and then, and only then, will they believe in what you have to say and what you have to offer. This module is going to be about connecting to your audience, finding ways, small and large, to get people to emotionally connect with you and believe and trust in the words you say. It is essential if you want to convince people that the things you have to say are worth listening to and remembering.
